chapter: Chapter 3: Electricity: Circuits and Their Components

questions.question: Explain how Ohm’s law is used to calculate current, voltage, and resistance in a circuit.
questions.answer: Ohm’s law states V = IR, where V is voltage, I is current, and R is resistance. Using any two known values, the third can be calculated: I = V/R, R = V/I.

questions.question: Draw a parallel circuit with three bulbs and explain how current divides among the branches.
questions.answer: In a parallel circuit, the current from the source splits into separate paths. Each bulb receives current based on its resistance. Total current is the sum of branch currents.

questions.question: Explain the effect of connecting resistors in series and parallel with calculations.
questions.answer: In series, total resistance R_total = R1 + R2 + R3… Current decreases as resistance increases. In parallel, 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3… Total resistance decreases, increasing total current.

questions.question: Explain how a fuse works and calculate the maximum current allowed for a 5 A fuse connected to a 220 V supply.
questions.answer: A fuse melts when current exceeds its rating, breaking the circuit. Maximum power through fuse: P = VI = 220 × 5 = 1100 W.

questions.question: Explain the concept of electromagnetism with an example of its use in everyday devices.
questions.answer: Electromagnetism occurs when current through a coil produces a magnetic field. Example: Electric bell uses an electromagnet to move the hammer and strike the gong.

questions.question: Draw a series circuit with a battery, two bulbs, and a resistor. Explain how total resistance and current are affected.
questions.answer: In series, total resistance R_total = R1 + R2 + R3. Increasing resistance decreases total current. Current is the same through all components, so bulbs glow dimmer with higher resistance.

questions.question: Explain why electricity from the mains is AC and batteries provide DC.
questions.answer: AC changes direction periodically, allowing efficient long-distance transmission (mains supply). DC flows in one direction, stored in batteries, ideal for small devices like torches and mobiles.

questions.question: Explain the difference between open and closed circuits and provide real-life examples.
questions.answer: Closed circuit: complete path, current flows, e.g., light turned on. Open circuit: path broken, no current, e.g., switch off in a room.

questions.question: Explain the effect of wire thickness on resistance and current in a circuit.
questions.answer: Thicker wires have lower resistance, allowing more current to flow. Thin wires have higher resistance, reducing current and potentially overheating.
answer_outline: Relationship between wire thickness and resistance

questions.question: Explain the importance of circuit breakers in modern electrical circuits.
questions.answer: Circuit breakers automatically cut off current in case of overload or short circuit, protecting appliances and preventing fire hazards.

questions.question: Explain the difference between a battery and a cell and provide examples of each.
questions.answer: A cell provides a single source of DC voltage, e.g., AA cell. A battery consists of multiple cells connected together to give higher voltage, e.g., car battery.

questions.question: Explain the importance of proper earthing in electrical systems.
questions.answer: Earthing provides a path for excess current to flow safely into the ground, preventing electric shocks and protecting appliances.

questions.question: Explain how adding more bulbs in series affects the brightness of each bulb with calculation.
questions.answer: Adding bulbs increases total resistance, reducing current (I = V/R). Reduced current lowers brightness of each bulb. Example: Two 6 Ω bulbs in series with 12 V battery: I = 12/(6+6) = 1 A.

questions.question: Explain how adding more bulbs in parallel affects the brightness of each bulb with calculation.
questions.answer: In parallel, voltage across each bulb remains same, so brightness does not change. Total current increases as more bulbs are added. Example: Each 6 Ω bulb with 12 V: I per bulb = 12/6 = 2 A.

questions.question: Explain why a high resistance wire heats up more than a low resistance wire for the same current.
questions.answer: Heat produced H = I²R. For same current, higher resistance produces more heat. Practical example: Electric heater wire has high resistance to generate heat.

questions.question: Explain why a parallel circuit is preferred for household wiring rather than a series circuit.
questions.answer: Parallel ensures each appliance gets full voltage and can work independently. In series, if one appliance fails, entire circuit stops working.

questions.question: Explain how an electric bell works using electromagnet and hammer mechanism.
questions.answer: When switch is pressed, current flows through electromagnet, attracting hammer to strike gong. Circuit breaks momentarily, hammer returns, cycle repeats producing ringing sound.

questions.question: Explain the importance of knowing circuit diagrams for repairing electrical devices.
questions.answer: Circuit diagrams show connections and components, helping locate faults, understand current flow, and safely repair devices.

questions.question: Explain how insulation thickness affects current leakage and safety.
questions.answer: Thicker insulation reduces leakage and prevents shocks, ensuring safety. Thin or damaged insulation increases risk of electric shock and short circuits.

questions.question: Explain the difference between potential difference across components in series and parallel circuits.
questions.answer: Series: voltage divides among components; V_total = V1 + V2 + … Parallel: voltage across each branch is same as source voltage.

questions.question: Explain the safety precautions to be taken while handling electrical appliances.
questions.answer: Switch off before repair, avoid wet hands, check insulation, do not overload circuits, use fuses and circuit breakers.

questions.question: Explain why connecting a high-wattage appliance without proper fuse is dangerous.
questions.answer: High-wattage appliance draws more current. Without proper fuse, wires may overheat causing fire or damage to appliance.

questions.question: Explain the principle of working of an electric fan using a motor and current supply.
questions.answer: Electric current passes through motor coil creating a magnetic field. Interaction with magnet rotates rotor, spinning fan blades to produce airflow.

questions.question: Explain why short circuits cause excessive current and how protective devices prevent hazards.
questions.answer: Short circuit provides a low-resistance path, increasing current. Fuses and circuit breakers cut off current to prevent damage and fire.

questions.question: Explain how knowledge of electricity and circuits helps in daily life and industries.
questions.answer: Helps in safe usage and repair of devices, designing circuits, preventing hazards, saving energy, and efficient operation of industrial machines.
